era_context: Alexander is consolidating his control of the Persian Empire. The major cities have fallen, and Darius III is dead. However, Bessus, the satrap of Bactria, has claimed the Persian throne and is leading a resistance movement in the eastern provinces. Alexander pursues Bessus and continues to expand his control. The year is marked by the capture of Persepolis, the symbolic heart of the Persian Empire, and by the continued pursuit of Bessus. The Achaemenid dynasty has effectively ended, and Alexander is establishing himself as the successor to the Persian kings.

events.title: Capture of Persepolis
events.description: Alexander moves toward Persepolis, the ceremonial capital of the Persian Empire and the location of the royal palaces. The city is defended by a Persian garrison, but the garrison is defeated and the city is captured. Alexander enters Persepolis and is recognized as the legitimate ruler. However, the city is subsequently burned. The burning of Persepolis is controversial; sources differ on whether it was deliberate or accidental, and on whether it was ordered by Alexander or occurred during a drunken celebration. The burning is significant because it symbolizes the destruction of the Persian Empire and because it demonstrates Alexander's willingness to destroy the symbols of Persian power. The destruction of Persepolis is also controversial because it destroys valuable archives and cultural artifacts. The capture and burning of Persepolis is a turning point in Alexander's campaign; it marks the effective end of the Persian Empire as a political entity.
key_figures: Ariobarzanes (Persian satrap of Persis, defends the city)
events.certainty_note: The capture of Persepolis is attested in all major ancient sources. The date (early 330 BCE) is secure. However, the burning of the city is controversial; sources differ on whether it was deliberate or accidental.

events.title: Death of Darius III
events.description: Darius III, the last Achaemenid Great King, is killed in Bactria. According to sources, Darius is captured by Bessus and his allies and is killed (sources differ on the exact circumstances; some claim he was killed by Bessus, others claim he was killed by his own guards or died of wounds). The death of Darius marks the effective end of the Achaemenid dynasty and the end of the Persian Empire as an independent power. Bessus claims the Persian throne for himself, adopting the title of Great King and the name Artaxerxes V. However, Bessus's claim is not widely recognized, and he is eventually captured and executed by Alexander. The death of Darius is significant because it removes the legitimate Persian ruler and because it forces Alexander to deal with Bessus as a rival claimant to the Persian throne.
key_figures: Darius III Codomannus (Persian Great King, killed)
Bessus (satrap of Bactria, reportedly kills Darius or orders his death)
events.certainty_note: The death of Darius III is attested in all major ancient sources. The date (330 BCE, summer) is secure. However, the exact circumstances of his death are disputed; sources differ on whether he was killed by Bessus, by his own guards, or died of wounds.

events.title: Alexander pursues Bessus into Bactria
events.region: Bactria and northeastern Persian Empire (modern Afghanistan)
events.description: After the death of Darius III, Alexander pursues Bessus, who has claimed the Persian throne and is leading a resistance movement in the eastern provinces. Alexander moves northeastward into Bactria, pursuing Bessus. The pursuit is difficult, as Bessus has support from local tribes and from the Scythian nomads. Alexander's campaign in Bactria is protracted and difficult, lasting several years. However, Bessus is eventually captured (in 329 BCE) and executed. The pursuit of Bessus is significant because it demonstrates Alexander's determination to eliminate all rivals to his rule and because it extends Alexander's campaign into Central Asia. The campaign in Bactria also demonstrates the limits of Alexander's power; the terrain is difficult, the local populations are resistant, and the supply lines are stretched thin.
events.certainty_note: The pursuit of Bessus is attested in multiple sources. The date (late 330 BCE onward) is secure. The campaign in Bactria extends into 329 BCE.

events.title: Alexander establishes himself as successor to Persian kings
events.description: After the death of Darius III and the capture of the major cities of the Persian Empire, Alexander establishes himself as the legitimate successor to the Persian kings. He adopts Persian customs and dress, and he is recognized as the Great King by the Persian nobility and the populations of the conquered territories. This consolidation is significant because it demonstrates Alexander's intention to rule the Persian Empire as a successor to the Achaemenids rather than as a foreign conqueror. Alexander's adoption of Persian customs is controversial among his Macedonian companions, some of whom view it as a betrayal of Macedonian traditions. However, Alexander's strategy of presenting himself as a legitimate Persian ruler is effective in consolidating his control of the empire.
key_figures: Alexander III of Macedon (establishes himself as Great King)
events.certainty_note: Alexander's adoption of Persian customs and his recognition as Great King are attested in multiple sources. The date (330 BCE onward) is secure.
cross_references: Part of broader consolidation of Alexander's rule

events.title: Burning of Persepolis: deliberate or accidental?
events.description: The burning of Persepolis is one of the most controversial events of Alexander's campaign. Sources differ on whether the burning was deliberate or accidental, and on whether it was ordered by Alexander or occurred during a drunken celebration. According to some sources (particularly Plutarch and Diodorus), the burning occurred during a drunken celebration, when Alexander and his companions were drinking and a woman named Thais suggested burning the palace. According to other sources (particularly Arrian), the burning was a deliberate act of revenge for the Persian invasion of Greece. The burning destroys valuable archives and cultural artifacts, and it is controversial because it represents the destruction of the symbols of Persian civilization. The burning is significant because it symbolizes the destruction of the Persian Empire and because it demonstrates Alexander's willingness to destroy the symbols of Persian power.
key_figures: Alexander III of Macedon (orders or permits the burning)
Thais (woman who allegedly suggests burning the palace)
events.certainty_note: The burning of Persepolis is attested in multiple sources. The date (330 BCE) is secure. However, the exact circumstances and motivation are disputed.

disconfirming_evidence: The burning of Persepolis is controversial, and sources differ on whether it was deliberate or accidental. Arrian claims it was a deliberate act of revenge, while Plutarch and Diodorus claim it occurred during a drunken celebration. Modern scholars debate which account is more reliable. The story of Thais suggesting the burning is attested in some sources but is absent from others, suggesting it may be a later literary invention. The exact circumstances of Darius III's death are disputed; sources differ on whether he was killed by Bessus, by his own guards, or died of wounds. The claim that Alexander 'adopted Persian customs' is attested, but the extent and nature of this adoption are debated by modern scholars.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 330 BCE is rich but heavily dependent on sources written 300+ years after the events. The sources are generally favorable to Alexander and may exaggerate his achievements or minimize Persian capabilities. No contemporary Persian sources survive. The narrative is shaped by the triumphalist tradition of Alexander's campaign. The capture of Persepolis is well-attested and historically significant, but the burning of the city is controversial and the sources are inconsistent. The death of Darius III is well-attested, but the exact circumstances are disputed. The sources emphasize the dramatic personal confrontation between Alexander and Darius, which may be a later literary embellishment. The consolidation of Alexander's rule as successor to the Persian kings is attested, but the extent and nature of this consolidation are debated by modern scholars.

graph_edges.note: The capture of Persepolis led to the death of Darius III.
The death of Darius III prompted Alexander to pursue Bessus.
The capture of Persepolis enabled Alexander to establish himself as successor to Persian kings.
The burning of Persepolis was part of the capture of the city.
The pursuit of Bessus led to his eventual capture in 329 BCE.
